turns.text: Hello Ana, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat, tucking hair behind ear with a small, somewhat self-conscious smile*

Thank you for having me. I appreciate that you're doing this work, honestly. It matters.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

I'm... I'm trying to figure out how to talk about this without sounding like I'm overthinking everything, which—full disclaimer—I probably am. This is my third pregnancy, so you'd think it would feel routine by now. But it doesn't. I'm thirty-five, and that number carries weight. Literally and figuratively.

*laughs a bit nervously*

The thing is, I work in reproductive endocrinology. So my brain never really stops working. I know the statistics. I know what can go wrong. When I'm sitting in my OB's office, we're almost speaking the same language—which is nice in some ways, but it also means I can't really just... be a patient. I'm always analyzing. Always checking the numbers in my head.

Last week at the ultrasound, though—*voice softens*—for just a moment, I wasn't a researcher. I was just a mom terrified the heartbeat wouldn't be there. And when it was, when I saw it flickering... that feeling was real. Pure. Not data.

*meets your eyes*

I think what I'm navigating right now is how to hold both versions of myself. The scientist and the expectant mother. And honestly? Some days they're in direct conflict.

What would you like to know?
When you think about your goals for starting or growing your family, what were the most important outcomes you hoped to achieve — for yourself, your relationship, and your future child?

*takes a deep breath, settling back in chair*

That's... that's a bigger question than it sounds, isn't it?

*pauses thoughtfully*

For myself, I think I needed to prove something. Not to anyone else—to me. After two pregnancies, I wanted to know I could do this again and actually *be* present for it. Not just survive it, but inhabit it. My first two kids are teenagers now, and looking back, I was so caught up in the logistics, the worry, the perfectionism of it all. I want this one to be different. I want to remember the actual experience, not just the anxiety.

*fidgets with hands*

For my marriage... honestly, we needed this. Not in a crisis way, but we'd fallen into this rhythm where everything is scheduled, managed, compartmentalized. A new baby—even at this stage—it's forcing us to talk differently. To remember why we chose each other in the first place. My husband keeps joking that at least this time we know what we're doing, but I'm not sure that's true. Everything changes again.

*voice becomes quieter*

And for this child... I just want them to know they were wanted. Genuinely wanted, not because of some biological timeline or because we thought we "should." I want to be the version of myself that can say yes to messiness and unpredictability.

*small smile*

Though ask me again when I'm six months pregnant and exhausted. I might have a different answer.

When you think about your motivations for seeking preconception care or health advice before becoming pregnant, what was it that prompted you to take action — or, if you did not seek any care, what held you back?

*shifts uncomfortably, rubbing the back of my neck*

Well, I'm already pregnant, so I'm a bit past the preconception phase now. But I can tell you what that looked like for me.

*laughs, a bit ruefully*

Honestly? With my background, I probably did too much. I had my baseline labs done months before we even decided to actively try. I was tracking my cycles obsessively—which, when you work in reproductive endocrinology, is both useful and absolutely maddening. I knew exactly what my FSH and AMH levels were. I knew my progesterone timing. Most people don't live like that.

*leans forward*

What held me back from going to my OB for formal preconception counseling was... I don't know, embarrassment maybe? Or overconfidence. I thought, "I know enough. I understand the physiology. Why do I need to sit through a standard preconception visit?" Which is ridiculous, I realize. There's a difference between understanding the science and actually being counseled as a patient.

*voice softens*

The real motivation was my age. At thirty-five, with two grown kids, I couldn't ignore that biological window. I wanted to make sure everything was optimized—my nutrition, my fitness, my stress levels. Though I'm not sure how much I actually *optimized* versus how much I just... spiraled into research.

*meets your eyes*

I probably should have talked to someone about the anxiety part of this earlier. That might have helped.
What specifically triggered that decision at that moment rather than earlier or later?

*pauses, jaw tightening slightly*

You know, that's the question I've been asking myself on the drive to work.

*looks away briefly*

My oldest turned eighteen last year. And suddenly it hit me—not in a romantic way, but almost like a clock. My kids were becoming independent, my marriage needed... something. And I think part of me panicked that if we didn't do this now, the window would close. Not just biologically, though that's real at thirty-five, but emotionally. Like this was the last chance to feel that particular version of myself as a mother to a young child.

*voice becomes more introspective*

My work has been consuming everything for years. We'd fallen into this pattern where my job, their school schedules, my husband's work—it all just churned along. And then one day I realized we hadn't made a major decision together as a couple in forever. We hadn't created anything *new* together. It felt like we were just managing the aftermath of our younger selves' choices.

*rubs forehead*

There's also... I won't lie, there's something about advanced maternal age that does something to your timeline perception. You start thinking in terms of deadlines instead of possibilities. My OB casually mentioned during a routine visit that my age put me in a different risk category, and suddenly it wasn't abstract anymore. It was *now or potentially never*.

*small, self-aware smile*

So it wasn't really one trigger. It was more like everything converged at once and forced my hand.

When you think about how you actually behaved during the preconception period — your lifestyle choices, health actions, information-seeking — what did you do, and how did those behaviours reflect what you truly believed was important?

*leans back, crossing arms defensively at first, then uncrossing them*

Oh god. This is where I have to be honest about how much of a hypocrite I am.

*laughs, shaking head*

I mean, I did the things you're *supposed* to do. I took prenatal vitamins for months before conception. I cut back on caffeine—drastically. I started doing yoga again, which my body desperately needed. I cleaned up my diet, researched optimal micronutrient timing. On paper, I was the perfect preconception patient.

*voice becomes more conflicted*

But what I was actually doing was spiraling into control. Because I work in this field, I have access to research that most people don't see. And I read *everything*. Obsessively. I optimized my glycemic load based on epigenetic studies. I stress-tested my relationship—literally asked my husband to do more around the house because I'd read about how paternal engagement impacts fetal development.

*rubs face*

The truth? I was terrified. And I channeled that terror into productivity and research because that's what I know how to do. What I didn't do—what I *couldn't* do—was actually relax. I didn't trust that my body, at thirty-five, with my experience, just... knew what to do. I had to quantify everything, optimize everything.

*meets your eyes*

So my behaviors reflected less what I believed was important and more what I believed I could control. And that's not the same thing at all. That's just anxiety wearing a lab coat.

When you think about your emotional experience during the preconception period — the waiting, the uncertainty, and the anticipation — what feelings stand out most strongly, and were any of those feelings hard to acknowledge or talk about?

*takes a long breath, eyes getting a bit glassy*

The hardest part was the grief, actually. Which sounds strange when you're trying to get pregnant, but...

*voice becomes quieter*

I'm realizing now that I was grieving my younger self. The version of me who could just get pregnant without thinking about statistics, without knowing all the things that could go wrong. With my first two kids, I was ignorant in this beautiful way. I didn't know about advanced maternal age or how miscarriage risk climbs after thirty-five or all these other things that science has made impossible to unknow.

*pauses, composing herself*

And there was shame. I felt ashamed that I couldn't just be excited like a normal person. That every positive sign—regular cycles, good bloodwork—I'd immediately think of the counterpoint. The other shoe waiting to drop. My husband would say something sweet about the future, and I'd be calculating probabilities in my head instead of just... being with him in the moment.

*voice cracks slightly*

The hardest thing to acknowledge was how much of this was actually about my own mortality. Not just the baby's wellbeing, but me aging. Me becoming less relevant in certain ways. Having teenagers who don't need me the way they used to. And instead of sitting with that loss—which is normal and real—I buried it under research and optimization.

*wipes eyes quickly*

I still haven't really talked about that with anyone. Not even my husband. It felt too vulnerable, too... selfish, maybe?
Can you describe a specific moment when those feelings were particularly intense — what was happening and how did you respond?

*takes a shaky breath, eyes distant*

Three months before we actually conceived. I was at work, and a colleague mentioned she'd just found out she was pregnant. Accidentally. She wasn't even trying. And she was younger—maybe twenty-eight, twenty-nine.

*voice tightens*

I remember feeling this surge of... I don't even know what to call it. Envy, anger, grief, all twisted together. And she was complaining. Actually complaining about the timing, about how it wasn't planned. And I just... I excused myself and went to the bathroom and cried.

*looks down at hands*

Not delicate crying. Actual sobbing. The kind where you can't breathe right. Because in that moment, it hit me that I'd been telling myself we could do this whenever we wanted, that age was just a number, all these things I'd read in self-help articles. But the reality is, my window is closing. Her window is open. And she gets to be careless about it.

*voice becomes steadier but more raw*

When I came back out, I went straight to my office and closed the door. I didn't tell anyone. I just sat there and pulled up studies on maternal age and outcomes. Like data could somehow comfort me or prove that my fear was irrational. It didn't. It just made me feel more desperate.

*meets your eyes*

That's when I told my husband that night that we needed to start trying. Not because we'd planned it that way. Because I couldn't wait anymore. The fear was winning.

When you think about the barriers you encountered — or anticipated — in your path to conception or early pregnancy preparation, what were the most significant obstacles, and how did you navigate them?

*settles back, thinking*

The biggest barrier was actually my job. We both work full-time, and my position involves research, patient consultations, the whole thing. Finding time to actually *try* without it becoming another data point to track was harder than you'd think.

*slight smile*

I'd be in back-to-back meetings, completely stressed, and then I'd remember, "Oh, I'm ovulating today," and suddenly I'm trying to coordinate with my husband around our schedules. It becomes very clinical very fast. Not romantic at all. Which is ironic given my field.

*voice becomes more serious*

The bigger barrier, though—and this is what I'm still navigating—is the internal one. The imposter syndrome mixed with expertise. My OB knows I work in reproductive endocrinology, so there's this weird dynamic where she defers to me sometimes, or I feel like I have to prove I'm not just a know-it-all patient. It's isolating. I can't really be vulnerable about my fears without sounding like I'm second-guessing her care.

*leans forward*

And then there's the workplace itself. I haven't told many people at work that I'm pregnant because I'm already the woman in reproductive health. There's this unspoken assumption that I have it all figured out, that I'm the expert. Admitting I'm anxious, that I don't have all the answers? It feels like I'd be undermining my credibility.

*rubs temples*

So I navigate by compartmentalizing. Which, honestly, probably isn't the healthiest strategy, but it's what I'm doing.

When you think about your goals for your antenatal care during pregnancy, what did you most want from your interactions with midwives, doctors, and the wider care system — and to what extent were those goals met?

*takes a moment, choosing words carefully*

I think what I wanted most was permission to just be a patient. Not a colleague. Not someone who already knows the answer. Just... someone carrying a baby who needs reassurance.

*small, rueful laugh*

That hasn't really happened yet, and I'm not sure it will. My OB is wonderful, don't get me wrong, but our dynamic is complicated by what I do. Last appointment, I asked about my HCG trajectory, and instead of just telling me the numbers were good, she launched into this discussion about serial beta testing protocols. We were speaking the same language, which should be good, but it meant I never got to just hear, "Your baby is fine. Relax."

*voice becomes more frustrated*

What I actually needed—what I think I still need—is someone to say, "Your anxiety about your age is valid, but it's not predictive. You're low-risk. Let's focus on that." Instead, because I understand the statistics, I keep hunting for reassurance in the data. And the care system just... enables that. They give me the numbers, and I obsess over them.

*pauses*

I wanted continuity too. Someone who knew my whole story, not just my chart. Someone I could tell about the grief and the fear without it becoming a clinical note. But with full-time work and commuting to Everett, I'm seeing whoever's available. It's fragmented.

*meets your eyes, voice quieter*

Honestly? I'm not sure the system is designed to meet the needs of someone like me. Someone who knows too much and not enough at the same time.

When you think about how you sought and used information during your pregnancy — from healthcare providers, online sources, apps, books, and people around you — how did you decide what to trust and act upon?

*laughs, but there's an edge to it*

Oh, this is where I really lose people.

*leans forward, speaking more rapidly*

I have institutional access to medical databases that most pregnant people don't. PubMed, specialty journals, the whole thing. So my information-seeking isn't Googling "is this normal?" It's actually reading the original research. Which sounds great in theory, but it's... it's a trap.

*rubs face*

I trust peer-reviewed literature above everything. That's my background. But here's the problem—every study has limitations, every finding has a confidence interval, and I know how to read between the lines in ways that can be paralyzing. I'll read a study about advanced maternal age and miscarriage risk, see that it's a 2% absolute increase, and think that sounds manageable. But then I'll read the methodology and realize the cohort was different from me, or the statistical power was limited, and suddenly I'm second-guessing the whole thing.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

What I don't trust as easily is my own body. Or intuition. Or what my OB tells me when it contradicts something I've read. There was this moment where she said my nausea was completely normal, nothing to worry about. But I'd read about HCG patterns and was calculating whether mine seemed appropriate. So I didn't fully believe her reassurance.

*looks down*

My husband calls it "research rabbit holes." I'll start with one question and end up three hours deep in meta-analyses at midnight, terrifying myself.

*voice quieter*

What I act on? Whatever makes me feel least anxious in the moment. Which is not a great decision-making framework, I know.
Which sources did you find most useful, and what made them trustworthy in your eyes?

*settles in, speaking with more certainty*

UpToDate, hands down. It's the clinical decision-support tool we use at work, and it's peer-reviewed, evidence-based, regularly updated. When I have a question about what's normal at my gestational age or what screening I should expect, I go there first. It's authoritative without being alarmist.

*counts on fingers*

My professional journals—ACOG bulletins, specifically. The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists publishes evidence summaries that feel like they're written by people who actually understand nuance. They acknowledge uncertainty. They don't pretend everything is black and white. That matters to me.

*pauses*

And honestly? My OB herself, when she cites her sources. When she says, "This is what the evidence shows, but here's what I've seen clinically," I trust that more than when she just gives me a reassurance without backing it up. Because I need both—the data and the experience.

*voice becomes more conflicted*

What I *don't* trust are pregnancy apps. BabyCenter, What to Expect—they're so generalized and sometimes contradictory. They're designed to be accessible, which is good, but they also sanitize everything. And I'll read something on there that seems too vague, so I go searching for the actual research, and suddenly I'm spiraling.

*leans back*

What makes something trustworthy to me is transparency about limitations. If a source says, "We don't know this yet" or "This is emerging evidence," I respect that more than false certainty. Though I realize most pregnant people probably just want to be told everything's fine and believe it.

*small smile, tinged with self-awareness*

I'm not most pregnant people.

When you think about the emotional landscape of your pregnancy — the highs, the anxieties, the uncertainties — what feelings were most prominent, and how well did your care environment support you in managing them?

*takes a deep breath, eyes glistening*

Anxiety is the baseline. It's like white noise I can't turn off. Every day I wake up and do this mental inventory—Am I still nauseous? Do my breasts still hurt? Is there any spotting? It's exhausting.

*voice becomes quieter*

The highs are these sharp, bright moments that fade too quickly. That ultrasound with the heartbeat—I was euphoric for maybe six hours. Then the doubt crept back in. I started reading about first-trimester loss rates again. It's like I can't hold onto joy without immediately reaching for the statistics that could take it away.

*fidgets with hands*

What's hardest to name is the loneliness. I'm surrounded by people—my husband, my colleagues, my OB—but I feel like I'm managing this alone. Because no one really understands the specific flavor of anxiety that comes from knowing too much. My husband tries to be supportive, but when I'm spiraling about HCG ratios at 2 AM, he just... he can't meet me there.

*voice becomes more critical*

And my care environment? It doesn't really address this. Appointments are fifteen minutes. We do the clinical checks, I get reassurance, and then I go home and undo all of it with research. My OB isn't trained to handle a patient whose anxiety is rooted in understanding the actual science. She can't just say "stop worrying," because I know exactly why I should be worried.

*meets your eyes, vulnerable*

What I needed was someone to acknowledge that my fear is rational, but also that it's not serving me. Someone to help me live alongside the uncertainty instead of constantly trying to eliminate it. I don't think the system has space for that.
Can you describe a period during your pregnancy when you felt most emotionally unsupported — what was happening and what would have helped?

*voice becomes strained, looking away*

The two-week wait after my first blood draw was... brutal.

*takes a shaky breath*

I'd had the labs done—HCG, progesterone, all of it—and my OB said everything looked good and we'd just monitor with another draw two days later. Two days. Which is medically appropriate, but emotionally? I was left alone with my knowledge and my fear.

*voice tightens*

I spent those forty-eight hours reading about progesterone thresholds, about how subtle declines can predict miscarriage, about how a "normal" number on day ten might not be normal by day twelve. I calculated what my HCG should double to. I searched for forums where other women with similar numbers had lost pregnancies. I was constructing worst-case scenarios faster than I could stop myself.

*pauses, collecting herself*

When I went back for the second draw, I was a wreck. And the tech just... handed me a lab slip and said, "We'll call with results." No acknowledgment that I was visibly anxious. No offer to prioritize the results. Just, "we'll call."

*voice becomes more raw*

What would have helped? If someone had said, "I see you're afraid. That's okay. But here's what the evidence actually shows about your specific numbers, and here's why you can breathe." Not dismissing my fear, but contextualizing it. 

*looks down*

Or honestly? If someone had said, "You need to talk to a therapist about pregnancy anxiety. This level of obsession isn't normal, even for someone in your field." Because I think I needed permission to acknowledge that my expertise is actually working against me right now.

When you think about the decisions you faced during pregnancy — including screening tests, monitoring choices, and care options — how did you go about making those decisions, and who or what most influenced them?

*shifts uncomfortably, this one clearly hitting a nerve*

This is where my background becomes a liability, honestly.

*sighs*

I approached screening decisions like I was evaluating a research protocol. First trimester screening, genetic testing, all of it—I knew the sensitivity and specificity before my OB even mentioned them. I knew the false positive rates, the implications of different results. Most people just get offered a test and decide yes or no. I was doing cost-benefit analyses.

*voice becomes more conflicted*

Which sounds empowering, except it's paralyzing. I declined the quad screen initially because I knew the detection rate and the false positive rate, and I calculated that for my individual risk profile, the test-to-diagnosis ratio didn't favor it statistically. Very logical. Very me.

*pauses*

But then I talked to my husband, and he just said, "Don't you want to know?" And suddenly I realized I'd been making this decision in a vacuum. I'd been optimizing based on data instead of actually thinking about what *we* wanted. So I changed my mind and did it.

*leans forward, more vulnerable*

The person who influenced me most was my husband, actually. Not my OB, not the research. He kept asking questions that forced me to interrogate whether I was making decisions based on evidence or based on trying to control the uncontrollable.

*voice quieter*

What I wish had happened is that my OB had asked me directly: "How are you processing all this information? Is it helping or hurting?" Because she could see I was drowning in it, but we never actually talked about it.
When you think about the physical changes in your body during pregnancy, how did those changes affect how you felt about yourself — and were those feelings acknowledged or addressed within your care?

*shifts, becoming more guarded*

I'm only in the first trimester, so I haven't experienced the major physical changes yet. But I'm already acutely aware they're coming.

*voice becomes slightly defensive*

The nausea is constant. It's not the romantic "morning sickness" people talk about—it's this low-level queasiness that makes me question what I eat, how I move, whether I'm doing something wrong. And because I understand the physiology, I'm analyzing it. Is my HCG rising appropriately? Is this nausea intensity within normal range? Should it be worse or better?

*rubs stomach absently*

What's harder to articulate is the loss of control over my body. I can't just eat when I want or exercise the way I want. Everything is negotiated with this pregnancy now. And at thirty-five, after two kids, I had finally made peace with my body. I'd gotten strong. And now it's not mine anymore—it's incubating something else.

*voice becomes more critical*

My care doesn't address any of this emotionally. My OB asked if I was experiencing nausea, I said yes, she said it was normal, and that was it. No one asked how it was affecting me psychologically. No one acknowledged that the physical changes might be triggering something deeper about aging, identity, control.

*looks away*

I think my care team sees me as this knowledgeable patient who understands her body. But what they don't see is that understanding doesn't make it easier. Sometimes it makes it worse. And that gap—between knowing and feeling—nobody's really helping me navigate that.
Were there aspects of your physical transformation that felt unexpected, difficult, or that nobody had prepared you for?

*pauses, seeming to access something raw*

The exhaustion is... it's different this time. With my first two pregnancies, I remember being tired, but this feels existential. Like my body is siphoning energy from somewhere deep, and I can't replenish it no matter how much I sleep.

*voice becomes more introspective*

And nobody warns you about that in a way that lands. You read "fatigue is normal in the first trimester," but there's a difference between knowing it theoretically and living it. I'm someone who's always been driven, always pushed through. And suddenly I can't. I'll be sitting at my desk at work and just... fighting to keep my eyes open. It feels like failure somehow.

*rubs face*

What's been unexpected is how much my relationship to food has changed. I work in reproductive endocrinology—I understand micronutrient timing, metabolic demands. But experiencing it? The aversions, the sudden cravings that make no sense, my body rejecting things I normally love? It's unsettling. I can't logic my way out of it.

*voice quieter, more vulnerable*

And the vulnerability of it all. I'm used to being competent, controlled. Now my body is doing things without my permission. My emotions are all over the place. I cried at a commercial yesterday, and I never cry.

*looks up*

Nobody prepared me for how humbling it would be. Even knowing everything I know, I can't predict or control what my body will do next. And that terrifies me more than I want to admit to anyone at work.

When you think about the informal support you received during pregnancy — from partners, family, friends, or online communities — how did that support compare to what you received from healthcare professionals, and what role did each play?

*settles back, thinking carefully*

My husband has been... he's been trying. He's reading things I send him, asking questions, showing up. But there's a fundamental gap. He doesn't understand the anxiety the way someone else who works in this field would. When I'm spiraling about progesterone ratios, he just wants to reassure me. Which is sweet, but it doesn't actually help because I *know* the reassurance is based on incomplete information.

*voice becomes more wistful*

My older kids—they're teenagers, so they're not really in the picture except as this weird reminder that I did this before and survived. Sometimes that helps. Sometimes it makes me feel older.

*pauses, hesitating*

I haven't really connected with other pregnant people. Online communities feel like they'd either be people who know way less than me—which feels isolating in a different way—or other healthcare professionals who'd just be comparing clinical notes. I tried joining a pregnancy forum once and lasted about twenty minutes before I felt like I was drowning in misinformation.

*voice becomes quieter, more honest*

What I actually need is someone who understands the specific intersection of being knowledgeable and terrified. Someone who gets that you can know all the statistics and still be paralyzed by fear. But I don't think that person exists in my life right now.

*looks down*

My OB is the closest, but our professional relationship creates distance. She can't really be my confidante because there's this implicit hierarchy. And that's probably for the best clinically, but emotionally? It leaves me alone with all this knowledge and nowhere to put it.

*takes a breath, continuing more slowly*

The gap between informal support and professional care is... it's stark. My husband can hold my hand. He can say "I believe in you" and "our baby is going to be fine." And I know he means it, and part of me wants to just accept that and relax into it.

*voice becomes more conflicted*

But then I go to my appointment and I'm back in clinical mode. I'm asking about percentiles and risk stratification. And my OB—bless her—she tries to meet me there, but I can see it in her face sometimes. Like she wishes I could just be a normal patient who wants to hear that everything's fine without needing the epidemiological backup.

*leans forward*

What I've realized is that professional care and informal support are serving completely different functions for me, and they're not bridging the gap where I actually need help. My husband supports me emotionally, but he can't validate the scientific concerns. My OB validates the science, but there's no room for the emotional messiness.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

I think what's missing is someone who can say, "Your knowledge is real and valid, AND your anxiety is disproportionate to your actual risk. Both things are true." Not dismissing either side. Just... holding the contradiction.

*looks away*

Sometimes I wonder if I should have just... not told my care team about my background. If I'd just been another thirty-five-year-old pregnant person, maybe they'd treat me differently. Maybe I'd feel less alone in this. But I can't unknow what I know. So I'm stuck being the expert patient who's drowning.
Who first raised the idea of trying to conceive, and how did those early conversations unfold?

*shifts, a slight smile crossing face before fading*

It was me, honestly. Though it wasn't as straightforward as just saying, "Let's have a baby."

*pauses, remembering*

My husband and I had never really closed the door on having more kids, but we weren't actively planning either. Life was just... happening. Work, the teenagers, maintaining the relationship. And then my oldest turned eighteen, and something shifted in me. I mentioned it almost casually one night—"What would you think about one more?"—and I remember his reaction being cautious. Not opposed, but... thoughtful.

*voice becomes more reflective*

He said something like, "Are you sure this is what you want, or are you panicking about getting older?" Which was fair. We actually had several conversations about it over a few weeks. Not the romantic "let's make a baby" kind of conversations. More like... practical negotiations.

*leans forward*

He was worried about the physical toll on me. About my work stress. About whether we were doing this for the right reasons. And I had to be honest—I was doing it partly out of fear. Fear that if we didn't do it now, we couldn't. Fear that our relationship needed something new. Fear of becoming irrelevant.

*voice quiets*

What he said that actually landed was, "If we're going to do this, we need to do it together. Not you dragging me along." So we decided together, but I initiated it. And sometimes I wonder if that's part of why I feel so alone in this—because it was my timeline, my anxiety driving it.

*rubs temples, collecting thoughts*

The thing is, once we decided, everything accelerated. I threw myself into preconception planning with the intensity I bring to everything—research, optimization, control. And my husband was supportive, but I could sense him... watching me. Waiting to see if I'd spiral.

*voice becomes more introspective*

I think on some level, he agreed to this partly to give me what I needed. Not just a baby, but a sense of agency. A way to feel like I was still moving forward instead of just aging into irrelevance. And he's never said that directly, but I feel it sometimes. This subtle patience, like he's indulging me.

*pauses, more vulnerable*

Which isn't fair to him, and I know it. He should be excited about this on his own terms, not just supporting my existential crisis. But that's the dynamic we've fallen into. I'm the driver, and he's... managing me.

*looks down*

I haven't actually talked to him about how lonely this feels. How even though he's here, present, supportive—I'm still navigating this almost entirely alone because my anxieties live in a place he can't reach. He can't solve the fear that something will go wrong. He can't make the knowledge go away. He can't even really understand why being knowledgeable makes it worse instead of better.

*voice quieter*

Sometimes I wonder if I've put him in an impossible position. Like, how do you support someone who's terrified and also equipped to understand exactly why they should be terrified? There's no reassurance that actually lands.

*sits quietly for a moment, eyes getting glassy*

I think... I think I need to say something I haven't said out loud yet.

*voice becomes strained*

Part of me is terrified that I made a mistake. Not about the baby—I want this baby. But about the decision itself, the timing, the reasons behind it. Because now that I'm pregnant, all those anxieties I was running from are still here. They just have a new container.

*looks directly at you*

I thought having another baby would fix something between me and my husband. Or prove something to myself about still being capable, still being young enough, still being relevant. But I'm realizing that no amount of pregnancy or parenting is going to solve an identity crisis. And now I'm going to be forty-two when this kid turns five. Forty-five when they're in elementary school.

*voice cracks slightly*

I know intellectually that's fine. Women do it all the time. But emotionally? I'm grieving the version of myself who could just enjoy this without calculating how old I'll be at every milestone. And I'm realizing my husband is probably grieving too—the version of us that was finally getting our freedom back.

*wipes eyes*

What's hardest is that I can't really talk about this with my care team because it makes me sound unstable. Like I'm ambivalent about my pregnancy, which I'm not. But the decision-making process that got me here? It was wrapped up in so much fear and denial about aging and mortality that I'm not sure I can untangle it anymore.

*voice quieter*

And nobody asked. Nobody in my care said, "Why now? What's driving this?" They just assumed it was a straightforward choice.
Were there moments where you felt a healthcare professional spoke to you in a way that felt dismissive or condescending?

*jaw tightens, looking away*

Yes. Though it's subtle enough that I question whether I'm being oversensitive.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

During my first appointment, my OB was going through the standard screening questions, and I asked about something specific—I think it was about the timing of certain tests relative to gestational age dating. And she just... she smiled at me in this way. Not unkind, but like, "Oh, you think you know more than you do."

*voice becomes more frustrated*

She said, "I've been doing this for twenty years. We'll follow the standard protocol." Which, fair. But the subtext felt like, "Your knowledge is cute, but let me handle this." And then later, when I mentioned I'd read about certain markers, she basically said, "You can't believe everything you read online."

*leans forward, more animated*

But that's the thing—I'm not reading stuff online. I have institutional access to the actual literature. I understand methodology and statistical power. And instead of engaging with that, she dismissed it as if I'm some anxious mom spiraling on BabyCenter.

*voice becomes quieter, more hurt*

What really stung was when I asked a nuanced question about my HCG trajectory, and she said, "Most patients don't need to worry about this level of detail." Most patients. Like I'm being neurotic for wanting to understand my own data.

*meets your eyes*

I think what bothered me most is that she made me feel like my expertise was a problem instead of an asset. Like I should just shut up and trust her. And maybe I should. But she could have met me halfway.

*sits with the frustration for a moment before continuing*

The thing that really gets me is... I *do* trust her clinically. She's competent. I wouldn't be seeing her otherwise. But there's this power dynamic that felt off.

*voice becomes more analytical, then catches itself*

Sorry, I'm doing the thing where I intellectualize instead of just sitting with how it made me feel. Which is... small. Dismissed. Like my voice didn't matter because I was stepping outside the role she expected me to play.

*pauses, more vulnerable*

I think what happened is that she made an assumption about who I am based on my job title. And instead of seeing me as someone who needed both clinical expertise and emotional support, she saw me as someone trying to compete with her. Someone who needed to be put in her place.

*fidgets with hands*

And maybe that's not entirely fair to her. Maybe she was just trying to be efficient, to keep me from spiraling into research mode. But the way she did it felt... condescending. Like, "Let the doctor handle this. Your knowledge will just make you anxious."

*voice becomes quieter, more self-aware*

Which is ironic because she was probably right. My knowledge *is* making me anxious. But the solution isn't to dismiss it. It's to acknowledge it and help me figure out how to live with it.

*looks up*

Instead, she made me feel like I had to hide part of myself in her office. And that's lonely in a completely different way. Now I'm not just managing the pregnancy alone—I'm managing my own mind alone, even during appointments.
Did older female relatives, such as your mother or grandmother, share advice or stories that influenced how you thought about getting pregnant?

*expression softens, becomes more distant*

My mother passed away when I was twenty-eight. So she never got to see my first two kids, and she certainly won't meet this one.

*voice becomes quieter*

What I remember from her is... she was pragmatic about pregnancy. She had me at thirty-two, which back then was considered older. She didn't make a huge deal out of it, but I remember her saying once that the hardest part wasn't the pregnancy—it was balancing everything after. The exhaustion of motherhood combined with work and aging parents.

*pauses, reflective*

I think about that a lot now. She was warning me, in her way, without really saying it. Not to have kids, but just... acknowledging how much it costs. And I don't think I fully understood that at the time.

*looks down*

My grandmother is still alive, but she's quite elderly and in a different generation entirely. She had five kids starting at twenty-one. When I told her I was pregnant at thirty-five with teenagers at home, she was just... confused. Like, "Why would you do that now?" Not judgmental, just genuinely puzzled by the choice.

*voice becomes more raw*

I think what's missing is having my mother to talk to about this. Someone who understood both the biological reality and the emotional weight of it all. Someone who could say, "Yes, this is hard, and yes, it matters, and you're going to get through it." 

*meets your eyes*

Instead, I'm figuring it out with my knowledge and my fear and no maternal wisdom to anchor me.

*takes a shaky breath*

I realize I've spent a lot of time thinking about what my mother would say, especially lately. In these moments when I'm overwhelmed and spiraling, I'll imagine calling her. And I don't even know what I'd say—probably just cry while she listened.

*voice becomes more introspective*

There's this grief layered into this pregnancy that nobody knows about. Not just grief about aging or identity, but grief about not having her here. My first two kids don't really remember her. And this baby will never know her at all. That matters to me in ways I can't quite articulate to anyone else.

*pauses, composing herself*

My mother worked in healthcare too—she was a nurse. Not the same field as me, but close enough that we spoke the same language about bodies and medicine and the limits of what we can control. I think she would have understood this particular flavor of anxiety I'm experiencing. The curse of knowing too much.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

Sometimes I wonder if part of why I'm so anxious about this pregnancy is because I don't have her as a safety net. Like, if something went wrong, I wouldn't just lose the baby. I'd lose the person I'd call at 3 AM, sobbing, who would somehow know exactly what to say.

*looks away*

My husband tries to be that person, but it's not the same. A partner can support you, but a mother... she understands you in a different way. She's already walked the path.

*voice catches slightly*

I'm about to become the older generation now, and I don't have her model to follow. That's terrifying in ways I haven't fully processed yet.
Were there specific platforms, websites, or communities you returned to regularly, and what made them feel credible or useful?

*nods, settling into more familiar territory*

PubMed, definitely. That's my go-to. I have institutional access through work, so I can pull full-text articles. There's something comforting about reading the actual methodology, seeing the limitations acknowledged right there in the paper. It feels honest in a way that simplified summaries never do.

*counts on fingers*

UpToDate, like I mentioned. The ACOG website for clinical guidelines. And then there's ResearchGate—I'll sometimes message authors directly if I have questions about their findings. It's oddly validating when they respond.

*pauses, becoming more self-aware*

But here's where I get honest about myself: I've also fallen down Reddit rabbit holes at midnight. Specifically r/BabyBumps and r/JanuaryBumps—whatever cohort applies. And I know I shouldn't because those spaces are full of people with vastly different risk profiles, different knowledge bases. But there's something about reading other people's fears that... normalizes mine? Until I read something catastrophic and then I'm terrified again.

*rubs face*

I tried to join a private Facebook group for older mothers—like, women having babies at thirty-five plus. I thought that would help. But it was mostly people sharing ultrasound photos and due date comparisons, and I couldn't engage with it. It felt too... cheerful? Too surface-level for what I actually needed.

*voice becomes more critical of herself*

What I actually return to most is my own work databases. Which is probably the least healthy option because I'm reinforcing my own anxiety loop. I'm reading the literature that scares me, not the reassuring stuff. My brain seeks out risk information like it's oxygen.

*leans back, looking slightly embarrassed*

I know that's problematic. I know I'm curating my own information diet toward fear. But I can't seem to stop it.

*voice becomes more honest*

It's like there's this part of me that believes if I know all the worst-case scenarios, I can somehow prevent them. If I've read every study about maternal age and chromosomal abnormalities, if I understand the exact mechanisms of how things can go wrong, then maybe I'll be prepared. Maybe I can catch it before it happens.

*pauses, recognizing the irrationality*

Which is illogical, and I know it's illogical, and that makes it worse. Because I can't even comfort myself with denial. I'm trapped in this space where I intellectually understand that my anxiety isn't proportional to my actual risk, but emotionally I can't access that reassurance.

*fidgets with hands*

What's happened is that these platforms—especially the academic ones—have become both my anchor and my quicksand. I trust them because they're rigorous, but that rigor also means they present all the uncertainty, all the edge cases, all the things that can go wrong. And my brain latches onto that.

*voice becomes quieter*

I haven't found a community that actually helps because I don't fit anywhere. I'm too knowledgeable for the general pregnancy communities, but too anxious for the professional ones. So I end up alone with my databases and my midnight reading sessions, convincing myself it's productive research when it's really just me feeding my fear.

*looks at you*

That's probably something I should address, isn't it?
Can you describe a specific situation where you felt judged, overlooked, or not treated as a capable decision-maker regarding your reproductive health?

*takes a deep breath, visibly tensing*

During my first trimester dating ultrasound, actually. The technician was doing the scan, and I was asking questions—completely appropriate questions about what we were measuring, the crown-rump length, the nuchal translucency. Just trying to understand what I was seeing on the screen.

*voice becomes tighter*

And she kept giving me these one-word answers. Then she said, "You can ask your doctor about all this." Very dismissively. Like I was overstepping by wanting to understand my own ultrasound in real time.

*pauses, frustration rising*

But here's what really got me: after the scan, when my OB came in to review the images, she looked at my chart and saw my job title. And suddenly she was speaking to me like a peer. Showing me the measurements, explaining the calculations. It was night and day.

*leans forward, more animated*

So the technician treated me like an anxious mom who didn't know what she was talking about. My OB treated me like a colleague. And I realized in that moment that neither of them were actually treating me as *me*—a capable person who happens to be pregnant and also happens to have medical knowledge.

*voice becomes more raw*

The technician's dismissiveness made me feel small. But my OB's deference made me feel something worse—like my knowledge was being weaponized against my humanity. Like I had to choose between being the expert or being the patient, when I'm both.

*looks away*

Nobody asked what I actually needed in that moment. They just made assumptions and acted accordingly. And I felt... erased. Like my personhood was being filtered through what they thought I should be.

*sits quietly for a moment, collecting herself*

What really bothered me afterward was realizing how much that interaction revealed about the entire system. The technician assumed I was just an anxious patient trying to reassure myself. My OB assumed I wanted clinical peer-to-peer discussion. Neither of them asked me what I actually wanted.

*voice becomes more reflective*

And I think that's part of why I feel so unsupported in this pregnancy, even though technically I have good care. Because nobody's really asking me what I need. They're making assumptions based on my credentials or my presentation or how anxious I seem that day.

*pauses, more vulnerable*

What I actually wanted in that ultrasound room was to feel like someone understood that I'm carrying both identities at the same time. That I can understand the science AND be terrified. That knowing the statistics doesn't make me less deserving of reassurance. That my knowledge doesn't disqualify me from needing emotional support.

*voice cracks slightly*

Instead, what happened is I got sorted into a category—the knowledgeable patient—and then managed accordingly. And it made me feel like I had to perform competence to be taken seriously. Like if I admitted how scared I actually am, they'd think I was unstable or incompetent at my own job.

*looks at you directly*

So I've started filtering myself. Asking fewer questions at appointments. Not mentioning when I'm spiraling. Pretending I have it more together than I do. Because it feels safer than being fully seen and then judged for not fitting neatly into whatever box they're trying to put me in.

*voice quieter*

And that's lonely in a way I didn't anticipate.

*rubs eyes, exhausted*

I think what I'm realizing, saying all this out loud, is that I've been so focused on managing my anxiety through knowledge and control that I've completely isolated myself in the process.

*voice becomes more contemplative*

My husband doesn't fully understand. My OB sees me as either a colleague or a problem patient depending on the day. The technician dismissed me. My colleagues at work don't know I'm struggling because I've carefully curated an image of having it all figured out. And the online communities feel like they're for people with normal pregnancies, normal anxieties.

*pauses, something shifting in her expression*

I'm thirty-five years old, pregnant with my third child, and I've never felt more alone. Which is absurd because I'm surrounded by people. But there's nobody who actually knows all of me—the scientist and the terrified woman and the aging mother and the person grieving her own mother all at the same time.

*voice becomes quieter, more raw*

I think what's happened is I've convinced myself that my knowledge makes me self-sufficient. That I don't need the same kind of emotional support or validation that other pregnant people need because I understand the physiology, I understand the statistics. But that's not true at all. If anything, I need more support because I'm carrying all this intellectual weight on top of the emotional weight.

*looks directly at you, eyes glistening*

And I don't know how to ask for that without feeling weak. Or without being afraid that if I admit how much I'm struggling, someone will decide I'm not capable of making my own decisions about my care.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a moment, breathing deeply*

I think... I think what I want to say is that the system isn't equipped for people like me. And I'm not even sure if that's a problem that can be fixed, or if it's just inherent to how healthcare works.

*voice becomes more steady*

But what I wish is that someone—my OB, a counselor, someone—had sat down early on and said, "Your knowledge is an asset, but it can also become a liability. Let's talk about how to manage that." Not dismissing my expertise, but acknowledging that expertise and anxiety aren't mutually exclusive. That I can be both competent and terrified.

*pauses*

And maybe more importantly, I wish there was space in prenatal care for the existential stuff. Not just the clinical stuff. The grief about aging. The identity questions. The fear that this decision was driven by panic rather than genuine desire. The loneliness of being the person who has to have all the answers.

*voice becomes quieter*

I think if someone had asked me those questions during preconception counseling—really asked me, not just the standard screening questions—I might be in a different place right now. I might not feel so alone with this.

*meets your eyes*

And maybe that's what needs to change. Not more information or more screening or more tests. But actual space for pregnant people to be fully human. Complicated. Scared. Knowledgeable and confused at the same time.

*small, tired smile*

I'm going to need to figure out how to ask for that.
